title: Australian Dictionary of Biography

infobox.value: 1966–67 | Covered those Australians who lived in the period 1788–1850
1969–76 | Covered those Australians who lived in the period 1851–1890
1979–90 | Covered those Australians who lived in the period 1891–1939
1993–2002 | Covered those Australians who lived in the period 1940–1980
2007–2012 | Covered those Australians who died between 1981 and 1990
2021 | Covered those Australians who died between 1991 and 1995
2005 | Dealt with those Australians not covered by the original volumes

or **AuDB**) is a national co-operative enterprise founded and maintained by the [Australian National University](/wiki/Australian_National_University "Australian National University") (ANU) to produce authoritative biographical articles on eminent people in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia")'s [history](/wiki/History_of_Australia "History of Australia"). Initially published by Melbourne University Press in a series of twelve hard-copy volumes between 1966 and 2005, the dictionary has been published online since 2006 by the **National Centre of Biography** (NCB) at ANU, which has also published ***Obituaries Australia*** (**OA**) since 2010.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Not to be confused with [Dictionary of Australian Biography](/wiki/Dictionary_of_Australian_Biography "Dictionary of Australian Biography").

Australian Dictionary of Biography

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| First edition of volume 1 | |
| Language | English |
| Subject | [Biographies](/wiki/Biography "Biography") of notable Australians |
| Genre | [Encyclopedia](/wiki/Encyclopedia "Encyclopedia") |
| Published | [Carlton](/wiki/Carlton,_Victoria "Carlton, Victoria"), Victoria |
| Publisher | [Melbourne University Press](/wiki/Melbourne_University_Press "Melbourne University Press") |
| Publication date | 1966–2021 |
| Publication place | Australia |
| Media type | * Print (1966–2021) * Online (2006–present) |
| [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") | [978-0-522-84459-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-522-84459-7 "Special:BookSources/978-0-522-84459-7") |
| [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") | [70677943](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/70677943) |
| Website | [adb.anu.edu.au](https://adb.anu.edu.au/) [Edit this at Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q672680#P856 "Edit this at Wikidata") |

The ***Australian Dictionary of Biography*** (**ADB** or **AuDB**) is a national co-operative enterprise founded and maintained by the [Australian National University](/wiki/Australian_National_University "Australian National University") (ANU) to produce authoritative biographical articles on eminent people in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia")'s [history](/wiki/History_of_Australia "History of Australia"). Initially published by Melbourne University Press in a series of twelve hard-copy volumes between 1966 and 2005, the dictionary has been published online since 2006 by the **National Centre of Biography** (NCB) at ANU, which has also published ***Obituaries Australia*** (**OA**) since 2010.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The ADB project began operating in 1957,[[1]](#cite_note-ADBabout-1) although preparation work had been started in about 1954 at the Australian National University. An index was created that would be the basis of the ADB. [Pat Wardle](/wiki/Pat_Wardle "Pat Wardle") was involved in the work and, in time, she herself was included in the ADB.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Staff are located at the National Centre of Biography in the History Department of the Research School of Social Sciences at the Australian National University. Since its inception, 4,000 authors have contributed to the ADB and its published volumes contain 9,800 scholarly articles on 12,000 individuals.[[1]](#cite_note-ADBabout-1) Only 210 of them are [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians"), an imbalance which can be equated with what the anthropologist [Bill Stanner](/wiki/Bill_Stanner "Bill Stanner") has called the white “cult of forgetfulness" about Indigenous achievements.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Similar titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Similar titles")]

The ADB project should not be confused with the much smaller and older *[Dictionary of Australian Biography](/wiki/Dictionary_of_Australian_Biography "Dictionary of Australian Biography")* by [Percival Serle](/wiki/Percival_Serle "Percival Serle"), first published in 1949, nor with the German *[Allgemeine Deutsche Biographie](/wiki/Allgemeine_Deutsche_Biographie "Allgemeine Deutsche Biographie")* (published 1875–1912) which may also be referred to as ADB in English sources.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Another similar Australian title from an earlier era was [Philip Mennell](/wiki/Philip_Mennell "Philip Mennell")'s *[Dictionary of Australasian Biography](/wiki/Dictionary_of_Australasian_Biography "Dictionary of Australasian Biography")* (1892).

## General editors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: General editors")]

Since the project began there have been six general editors as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography&action=edit), namely:[[5]](#cite_note-5)

* Douglas Pike (1962–1974)
* Bede Nairn (1974–1984)
* [Geoff Serle](/wiki/Geoffrey_Serle "Geoffrey Serle") (1975–1987)
* John Ritchie (1988–2002)
* [Diane Langmore](/wiki/Diane_Langmore "Diane Langmore") (2001–2008)
* [Melanie Nolan](/wiki/Melanie_Nolan "Melanie Nolan") (2008–present)

## Publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Publications")]

### Hardcopy volumes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Hardcopy volumes")]

To date, the ADB has produced 19 [hardcopy](/wiki/Hardcopy "Hardcopy") volumes of biographical articles on important and representative figures in [Australian history](/wiki/Australian_history "Australian history"), published by [Melbourne University Press](/wiki/Melbourne_University_Press "Melbourne University Press"). In addition to publishing these works, the ADB makes its primary research material available to the academic community and the public.

| Volume(s) | Years published | Subjects covered |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 and 2 | 1966–67 | Covered those Australians who lived in the period 1788–1850 |
| 3 to 6 | 1969–76 | Covered those Australians who lived in the period 1851–1890 |
| 7 to 12 | 1979–90 | Covered those Australians who lived in the period 1891–1939 |
| 13 to 16 | 1993–2002 | Covered those Australians who lived in the period 1940–1980 |
| 17 and 18 | 2007–2012 | Covered those Australians who died between 1981 and 1990 |
| 19 | 2021 | Covered those Australians who died between 1991 and 1995 |
| Supplement | 2005 | Dealt with those Australians not covered by the original volumes |
| Index | 1991 | Index for Volumes 1 to 12 |

### Biographical Register

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Biographical Register")]

Two supplementary volumes were published as a by-product of the first 12 volumes of the *ADB*. These are *A Biographical Register, 1788–1939: Notes from the Name Index of the Australian Dictionary of Biography* (1987) in two volumes. These contain biographical notes on another 8,100 individuals not included in the *ADB*. Each entry contains brief notes on the individual concerned, gives sources, lists cross-references between entries and the *ADB* and there is an occupation index at the end of volume II.

### Online publication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Online publication")]

On 6 July 2006, the Australian Dictionary of Biography Online was launched by [Michael Jeffery](/wiki/Michael_Jeffery_(Australian_Army_officer) "Michael Jeffery (Australian Army officer)"), [Governor-General of Australia](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia"), and received a Manning Clark National Cultural Award in December 2006.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The website is a joint production of the ADB and the [Australian Science and Technology Heritage Centre](/wiki/Australian_Science_and_Technology_Heritage_Centre "Australian Science and Technology Heritage Centre"), [University of Melbourne](/wiki/University_of_Melbourne "University of Melbourne") (Austehc).

## *Obituaries Australia*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Obituaries Australia")]

*Obituaries Australia* (OA), a digital repository of digital obituaries about significant Australians, went live in August 2010, after operating as an in-house database for some time, using *[Canberra Times](/wiki/Canberra_Times "Canberra Times")* journalist and deputy editor [John Farquharson](/wiki/John_Farquharson_(journalist) "John Farquharson (journalist)")'s obituaries for its pilot. The National Centre of Biography encouraged the public to send in scanned copies of obituaries and other biographical material.[[7]](#cite_note-oa-7)

The fully searchable database also links the obituaries to important digitised records such as war service records, [ASIO](/wiki/ASIO "ASIO") files and [oral history](/wiki/Oral_history "Oral history") interviews, in libraries, archives and museums. and will link to a search on the name in [Trove](/wiki/Trove "Trove"), the [National Library of Australia](/wiki/National_Library_of_Australia "National Library of Australia")'s database of newspapers, library catalogue holdings, government gazettes and other material.[[7]](#cite_note-oa-7)

The database comprises obituaries about "anyone who has made a contribution to Australian life"; some have not even visited Australia but had political or business connections and interests. There are links between ADB and AO on each entry where articles exist on both databases.[[8]](#cite_note-aboutobit-8)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Main article: [Slavery in Australia](/wiki/Slavery_in_Australia "Slavery in Australia")

In 2018, [Clinton Fernandes](/wiki/Clinton_Fernandes "Clinton Fernandes") wrote that ADB is conspicuously silent on the slaveholder or slave profiting pasts of a number of influential figures in the development of Australia, including [George Fife Angas](/wiki/George_Fife_Angas "George Fife Angas"), Isaac Currie, [Archibald Paull Burt](/wiki/Archibald_Paull_Burt "Archibald Paull Burt"), [Charles Edward Bright](/wiki/Charles_Edward_Bright "Charles Edward Bright"), Alexander Kenneth Mackenzie, [Robert Allwood](/wiki/Robert_Allwood "Robert Allwood"), [Lachlan Macquarie](/wiki/Lachlan_Macquarie "Lachlan Macquarie"), Donald Charles Cameron, John Buhot, [John Belisario](/wiki/John_Belisario "John Belisario"), Alfred Langhorne, John Samuel August, and [Godfrey Downes Carter](/wiki/Godfrey_Downes_Carter "Godfrey Downes Carter").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The NCB subsequently launched its *Legacies of Slavery* project, which aims to expand coverage of people who had links to British slavery.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
